event: Portrait of Saint Ercolano (Herculanus) of Perugia
symbols: bishop's crosier — pastoral authority, the shepherd's staff tending the flock of God
mitre — the episcopal office and authority to teach and govern
book — the Scriptures or the teaching office of the bishop
red cape — the blood of martyrdom
description: A half-length portrait of a young bishop-saint against a dark green background. He wears a white mitre (bishop's hat), a red cape over a green chasuble with ornate clasps, and holds a closed book in one hand and a golden bishop's crosier (pastoral staff) in the other. A golden halo encircles his head. His expression is calm and direct. A stone ledge or parapet runs along the bottom of the composition. The rendering is smooth and precise.
theological_description: Saint Ercolano (Herculanus) was a bishop of Perugia who was martyred during the Gothic siege of the city in the sixth century. As a bishop-martyr, he embodies the dual call to shepherd the flock and to lay down one's life for the sheep, following the model of Christ the Good Shepherd: 'The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep' (John 10:11). The crosier he holds symbolizes the pastoral responsibility given to Peter and through him to all bishops: 'Feed my sheep' (John 21:17). The book represents the bishop's primary duty of preserving and teaching the apostolic faith, as Paul charged Timothy: 'Guard the good deposit entrusted to you' (2 Tim 1:14). The red of his cape recalls the blood of martyrdom, the ultimate witness that gives the Greek word martyria its meaning — testimony sealed with one's life.

scripture_refs.reason: The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep — the bishop-martyr following Christ's example
Feed my sheep — the pastoral commission symbolized by the crosier
Guard the good deposit — the bishop's responsibility to preserve the faith, represented by the book
Be faithful unto death — the bishop-martyr's ultimate fidelity
